figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods on MTIL benchmark (Order I) in terms of “Transfer”, “Avg”, and “Last” scores (%). We label the best methods with bold styles. The top block indicates the upper-bound solutions to adapt the CLIP on each task.
Table 6: Comparison of state-of-the-art CL methods on the CIFAR100 and Tiny-ImageNet in terms of “Avg.” and “Last” scores (%).

Table 2: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods on few-shot MTIL benchmark (Order I) in terms of “Transfer”, “Avg.”, and “Last” scores (%). We label the best methods with bold styles. The top block indicates the upper-bound solutions to adapt the CLIP on each task.

Table 10: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods on few-shot MTIL benchmark (Order II) in terms of “Transfer”, “Avg.”, and “Last” scores (%). We label the best methods with bold styles. The top block indicates the upper-bound solutions to adapt the CLIP on each task.

Figure 2: Overall framework of the proposed method. (a) DIMoE-Adapters. During training, a task-specific router and a set of experts are introduced to learn from Dt, while previously learned routers and experts are frozen. At inference, task-specific routers activate a sparse subset of experts for efficient prediction. (b) SCEE. At the training stage, SCEE integrates Dynamic Metrics Collection and Dynamic Expert Evolution to manage the expert pool. (c) PGES. At the inference stage, PGES performs task identification via task-specific prototypes and routes samples to either DIMoE-Adapters for classification or the frozen CLIP model for zero-shot inference.
Figure 7: Analysis of expert evolution in SCEE on Task 6 (Flowers).

Table 9: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods on MTIL benchmark (Order II) in terms of “Transfer”, “Avg”, and “Last” scores (%).We label the best methods with bold styles. The top block indicates the upper-bound solutions to adapt the CLIP on each task.

Table 11: Routing accuracy of PGES in the MTIL setting. Rows indicate the current training stage, while columns indicate target tasks. Entries on and below the diagonal correspond to seen-task routing accuracy, and entries above the diagonal indicate the proportion of unseen-task samples routed to learned experts.

Figure 5: Illustration of the classification accuracy changes as tasks are being learned on the MTIL in two orders. The dashed lines represent the results of zero-shot predictions for an unlearned task. At task 0, the initial CLIP model’s zero-shot accuracy is evaluated.
Table 8: Comparison of state-of-the-art methods on the DomainNet dataset in terms of “Transfer”, “Avg.”, and “Last” scores (%).
